narrative: You push through the undergrowth and feel a sharp familiar sting on your wrist...
interesting_fact: The sting injects histamine and other compounds including serotonin. Serotonin is often called the happy chemical but it is actually a stimulant — here it amplifies the histamine reaction. It breaks down before reaching the brain so it will not make you feel good, just stung.

questions.question: What plant is this?
options: Stinging Nettle
Dead Nettle
Ground Ivy
Cleavers
questions.correct: Stinging Nettle

questions.question: What is the common name of this plant?
options: Stinging Nettle
Stinging Ivy
Stinging Dock
Stinging Wort
questions.correct: Stinging Nettle

questions.question: What is the botanical name of Stinging Nettle?
options: Urtica dioica
Urtica urens
Lamium album
Rumex obtusifolius
questions.correct: Urtica dioica

questions.question: Where is Stinging Nettle typically found?
options: Almost anywhere — woodland edges, hedgerows and nitrogen rich disturbed ground
Only in deep shaded woodland
Rocky coastal paths and cliff tops
Dry chalky grassland in full sun
questions.correct: Almost anywhere — woodland edges, hedgerows and nitrogen rich disturbed ground

questions.question: Name a key identifying feature of Stinging Nettle
options: Dark green serrated leaves covered in hollow stinging hairs on square stems
Heart shaped leaves in groups of three
Kidney shaped leaves with rounded toothed edges
Narrow leaves in whorls of six to eight
questions.correct: Dark green serrated leaves covered in hollow stinging hairs on square stems

questions.question: Name another key identifying feature of Stinging Nettle
options: Leaves grow in opposite pairs and droop slightly at the tips
Leaves fold down at night and in bright sunlight
Single line of hairs running up one side of the stem
Strong minty smell when crushed
questions.correct: Leaves grow in opposite pairs and droop slightly at the tips

questions.question: What is Stinging Nettle used for? Select all that apply
options: Cooked like spinach or added to soups
Made into tea
Made into pesto or beer
Fibres used for cordage and textiles
Eaten raw as a salad leaf
correct: Cooked like spinach or added to soups
Fibres used for cordage and textiles

questions.question: What does Stinging Nettle taste like when cooked?
options: Similar to spinach
Sharp and lemony
Bitter and earthy like coffee
Sweet and mild like peas
questions.correct: Similar to spinach

questions.question: What are the risks or cautions with Stinging Nettle? Select all that apply
options: Stings on contact — neutralised by cooking or thorough crushing
Do not eat older leaves after flowering — contain cystoliths which irritate the kidneys
Only harvest young spring growth
Avoid large quantities in early pregnancy
Toxic to humans even when cooked
correct: Stings on contact — neutralised by cooking or thorough crushing
Do not eat older leaves after flowering — contain cystoliths which irritate the kidneys
Avoid large quantities in early pregnancy

questions.question: Which species could Stinging Nettle be confused with?
options: Dead Nettle (Lamium album) — similar leaves but no sting and white flowers
Ground Ivy (Glechoma hederacea)
Cleavers (Galium aparine)
Chickweed (Stellaria media)
questions.correct: Dead Nettle (Lamium album) — similar leaves but no sting and white flowers

questions.question: When does Stinging Nettle flower?
options: May to September — small greenish brown flowers
March to April — white star shaped flowers
October to November — yellow flowers
December to February — pale pink flowers
questions.correct: May to September — small greenish brown flowers

questions.question: What time of year is Stinging Nettle best harvested?
options: March to May — young spring growth before flowering
Midsummer when fully grown
Autumn after it has gone to seed
Winter when other plants have died back
questions.correct: March to May — young spring growth before flowering

questions.question: Is Stinging Nettle native to the UK?
options: Yes
No
Only in England and Wales
It was introduced by Roman settlers
questions.correct: Yes

questions.question: What was Stinging Nettle historically used to treat? Select all that apply
options: Hay fever
Arthritis and rheumatism
Broken bones
Anaemia

questions.question: How was Stinging Nettle applied medicinally? Select all that apply
options: As a tea or infusion
Cooked and eaten as a nutritious food
Fresh leaves applied directly to arthritic joints
Burned and inhaled as smoke
Ground into a powder and swallowed dry
correct: Cooked and eaten as a nutritious food
Fresh leaves applied directly to arthritic joints

questions.question: What does rubbing a dock leaf on a nettle sting actually do?
options: There is no chemical evidence it works — it is likely distraction or placebo
The dock leaf releases an antihistamine that neutralises the sting
The coolness of the leaf draws out the histamine
The dock leaf juice breaks down the serotonin in the sting
questions.correct: There is no chemical evidence it works — it is likely distraction or placebo

questions.question: What chemicals does the nettle sting inject?
options: Histamine and other compounds including serotonin
Oxalic acid and tannins
Cyanide and alkaloids
Saponins and phenolic acids
questions.correct: Histamine and other compounds including serotonin

questions.question: What is the life cycle of Stinging Nettle?
options: Perennial — returns every year
Annual — completes in one year
Biennial — takes two years to complete
Ephemeral — only lasts a few weeks
questions.correct: Perennial — returns every year

questions.question: What soil or habitat conditions does Stinging Nettle prefer?
options: Nitrogen rich moist soil — its presence indicates fertile ground
Dry sandy soil in full sun
Waterlogged acidic peat bogs
Rocky chalk downland
questions.correct: Nitrogen rich moist soil — its presence indicates fertile ground

questions.question: What plant family does Stinging Nettle belong to?
options: Urticaceae — the nettle family
Lamiaceae — the mint family
Rosaceae — the rose family
Asteraceae — the daisy family
questions.correct: Urticaceae — the nettle family

questions.question: What is the origin of the name Urtica?
options: From Latin meaning to burn or to sting
Named after the Roman general Urticus
From Greek meaning two leaves
From Celtic meaning hedge plant
questions.correct: From Latin meaning to burn or to sting

questions.question: What does Dioica mean in the botanical name?
options: Two houses — male and female flowers grow on separate plants
Double sting — referring to the two types of hair
Two seasons — referring to spring and autumn growth
Double leaf — referring to the opposite leaf arrangement
questions.correct: Two houses — male and female flowers grow on separate plants

questions.question: What did Roman soldiers reportedly do with Stinging Nettles in Britain?
options: Flogged themselves with nettles to keep warm in the cold climate
Used the fibres to make their uniforms
Brewed nettle beer to drink on campaign
Fed nettles to their horses for energy
questions.correct: Flogged themselves with nettles to keep warm in the cold climate

questions.question: Some herbalists claim crushed nettle juice soothes a nettle sting. Why might this work?
options: Crushing destroys the stinging hairs and releases juice containing anti-inflammatory compounds including quercetin
The juice contains antihistamine which directly neutralises the sting
The coldness of the juice draws out the histamine
There is no basis for this — it is purely placebo
questions.correct: Crushing destroys the stinging hairs and releases juice containing anti-inflammatory compounds including quercetin
